Art.4.1.business_description: Personal data means any information relating to an identified or identifiable natural person. The scope is deliberately broad — name, email, phone number, IP address, location data, cookie identifiers, and even combinations of information that together identify someone all qualify. The key test is identifiability: if the data can be linked back to a living individual, directly or indirectly, it is personal data. Information about companies or deceased persons is not personal data. A common misconception is that only obviously identifying data like names applies — pseudonymised data where the key is held separately still counts as personal data under the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR).

exact: any information relating to an identified or identifiable natural person
scenario: determining whether data is personal data
combinations of data that identify individuals

Art.13.business_description: When collecting personal data directly from individuals, the controller must provide a defined set of information at the point of collection — not later. The mandatory categories from Art.13.1 are: identity and contact details of the controller; contact details of the Data Protection Officer (DPO) if designated; purposes and legal basis for processing; legitimate interests pursued (if Art.6.1.f applies); and any recipients or categories of recipients. Art.13.2 adds: retention periods; rights of the data subject including withdrawal of consent; the right to complain to a supervisory authority; and whether provision of data is a statutory or contractual requirement. This is the legal basis for the privacy notice or layered privacy information shown at data collection points.

exact: at the time when personal data are obtained
identity and the contact details of the controller
purposes of the processing and the legal basis
practitioner: what to tell individuals when collecting data
scenario: what to include in a privacy notice

Art.83.4.business_description: The lower administrative fine tier under the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) applies to infringements of obligations imposed on controllers and processors under specific articles: Art.8 (children's consent), Art.11 (processing not requiring identification), Art.25–39 (the core controller and processor obligations including Data Protection Impact Assessments (DPIAs), records of processing, security measures, breach notification, and Data Protection Officer (DPO) requirements), Art.42–43 (certification bodies and monitoring bodies). The maximum fine is €10 million or 2% of global annual turnover — whichever is higher. This tier covers procedural and organisational failures; the higher Art.83.5 tier applies to violations of fundamental principles and data subject rights.

exact: 2% of the total worldwide annual turnover
obligations of the controller and the processor
scenario: failure to designate a DPO when required
DPIA not conducted for high-risk processing
